---
title: "Grub Failure on 13.04 EFI Installation"
date: 2013-05-02
forum: General Help
---

### Post by bj44 on 2013-05-02
Installed 13.04 like I did on previous releases but after restart I get Grub Rescue error. All seems to be OK as far as partitions go, ran Boot-Repair but still the same issue exists.
Ref:  [http://paste.ubuntu.com/5626684/](http://paste.ubuntu.com/5626684/)

Appreciate any help... 

TIA

---

### Post by oldfred on 2013-05-02
You have what looks like an old efi partition with efi boot files. But efi partitions are only in gpt partitioned drives.

Your drive is now MBR(msdos) not gpt.
Ubuntu will boot from gpt partitioned drives with BIOS or UEFI. 
Windows will only boot from gpt partitioned drives with UEFI.

Are you booting in BIOS/CSM mode from UEFI/BIOS and booting Boot-Repair in BIOS mode?
Your fstab still shows a efi entry? Which in MBR mode should not be.

Because Boot-Repair sees the efi files and the Microsoft efi files in the old efi partition it thinks you still have an UEFI boot system.

---

### Post by bj44 on 2013-05-02
I don't have Windows 7 OS any longer and as you mentioned perhaps this is an old EFI. However I made sure I have deleted every single partitions before installation and recreated them under Ubuntu. This is very strange indeed, not sure how to get around it. The BIOS is set to EFI mode.

Thanks for the hint...

---

### Post by oldfred on 2013-05-02
You should just be able to go into the old efi partition and delete the files & folders in it.

Then make sure you are booting in BIOS mode not UEFI.

---

### Post by bj44 on 2013-05-02
Thanks, but I guess that is one way to get around it. I really wanted to be able to boot in UEFI mode to to take advantage of the firmware pluses. I did that in 12.10 installation and it worked perfect. Maybe 13.04 won't allow UEFI mode for single Ubuntu installation, perhaps you must have a secondary OS such as Win 7 or Win 8...
 
Thanks again for your help...

---

### Post by oldfred on 2013-05-02
It should install in UEFI mode. But how it installs is totally determined by how you boot installer.

You have to have secure boot off, if you have that setting, UEFI on, and CSM off and then boot live flash drive in UEFI mode. It should actually give two choices, UEFI or BIOS/CSM/Legacy or whatever.

If you want to partition in advance.
        I have created my gpt partitions with gparted. Under device, create partition table, advanced, choose gpt not the default msdos (MBR) partitioning.

      
 For the Total space you want for Ubuntu:
Ubuntu's standard install is just / (root) & swap, but it is better to add another partition for /home if allocating over 30GB.:
Only if gpt -  all partitions in gpt are primary:
gpt: 250 MB efi FAT32 (for UEFI boot or future use for UEFI, you only can have one per drive, so if already existing do not attempt another)
gpt: 1 MB bios_grub no format (for BIOS boot not required for UEFI)
gpt or MBR(msdos)
Ubuntu partitions - smaller root only where hard drive space is limited.
If total space less than about 30GB just use / not separate /home or standard install.
1. 10-25 GB Mountpoint / primary or logical beginning ext4(or ext3)
2. all but 2 GB Mountpoint /home logical beginning ext4(or ext3)
3. 2 GB Mountpoint swap logical

   Depending on how much memory you have you may not absolutely need swap but having some is still recommended. I do not hibernate (boots fast enough for me) but if hibernating then you need swap equal to RAM in GiB not GB. And if dual booting with windows a shared NTFS partition is also recommended. But you usually cannot create that as part of the install, just leave some space. Or partition in advance (recommended).
One advantage of partitioning in advance is that the installer will use the swap space to speed up the install. Thanks Herman for the tip.
[https://help.ubuntu.com/community/DiskSpace](https://help.ubuntu.com/community/DiskSpace)
[http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=2021534](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=2021534)

---

### Post by oldfred on 2013-05-02
Partitioning looks fine. Just realize whatever partitions you now have after some use you may want to change.

I cannot tell for sure, but since efi partition is mounted are you in UEFI mode?

Boot-Repair give lots of details. Post a new link.
 Post the link to the BootInfo report that this creates. Is part of Boot-Repair:
[https://help.ubuntu.com/community/Boot-Info](https://help.ubuntu.com/community/Boot-Info)

Yes after testing it this morning 13.04 failed to boot up. Boot-Repair does not work correctly either. I am back on 12.10 with EFI boot and all is working fine. I'll keep this thread open till this issue is resolved.

Thanks again for your support....

---

### Post by oldfred on 2013-05-03
It looks like have both BIOS on UEFI installs. Probably only on is configured correctly to boot.

You should grub in gpt's protective MBR and the efi partition with grub boot files.

So depending on settings in UEFI/BIOS you may be booting one way or the other.

---

### Post by bj44 on 2013-05-10
Not sure what you mean by [COLOR=#000000]grub in gpt's protective MBR. I did upgrade from 12.10 to 13.04 and it is working fine. As I mentioned before, when I install from 13.04 CD or USB it fails. I have attached a copy of my BIOS and as you see under Boot Configuration, UEFI Boot [Enabled]. Also note Boot Option #1 ===>>> is set to [ubuntu]. Ground up Installation from 13.04 CD will not give me this option. Any idea why that is? [/COLOR]

---

### Post by oldfred on 2013-05-10
The only reason I can think of is that you are not booting Live installer in UEFI mode. It installs in mode (UEFI or BIOS) you boot from.
 Is the 13.04 CD a CD, it is too large and needs to be a flash drive or DVD. And you have to be sure to download the 64 bit version as the 32 bit only installs in BIOS mode.

If you get a standard grub menu then you are booting installer in UEFI mode as it uses grub to boot.
If you get the accessibility screen (tiny keyboard & person at bottom of screen) then you are in BIOS mode as it boots with syslinux.

---

### Post by bj44 on 2013-05-14
Yes you are correct, it is a 64 bit DVD and I boot in UEFI mode ( no [COLOR=#000000]tiny keyboard & person at bottom of screen ). Perhaps my DVD is not good, Indeed it is very strange, I have done this with a Flash Drive as well with the same result. 
I'll repeat the process again -- get the new 64 bit ISO, burn to a new DVD and re-install. I'll report back after I done that.

Thanks again for support. [/COLOR]

---

### Post by oldfred on 2013-05-14
Are you booting but just get a black screen? The first time that happened to me, I only knew it was still booting as USB flash drive was still flickering & I knew it was still loading, but it had turned monitor off. I have nVidia and have had to use nomodeset since about 10.04 for all live installs and on first boot or until I install the nVidia proprietary driver.

With UEFI you boot with grub, so adding nomodeset is like first boot or you have to edit linux line in grub using e to get to entire boot stanza in grub menu.

       How to set NOMODESET and other kernel boot options in grub2 - both liveCD & first boot, but different 
[http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1613132](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1613132)
[https://help.ubuntu.com/community/BootOptions](https://help.ubuntu.com/community/BootOptions)
Graphics Resolution- Upgrade /Blank Screen after reboot  mega thread -  MAFoElffen
[http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1743535](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1743535)

---

### Post by bj44 on 2013-05-16
No, I do not have any graphics issue or black screen. Installing Ubuntu 12.10 adds a third Option which is called "ubuntu" to the options for booting (the other 2s are being from DVD and Hard Drive). Please have a look at the previous picture.
Now after the installation of 13.04 which almost exactly the same steps, I do not get that third Option. Not sure why that is, for now I'll go ahead and mark this as Solved. What I really ended up doing was installed 12.10 UEFI mode successfully then Upgraded to 13.04. It is running just fine for now. Thanks again for all your support...

---

### Post by oldfred on 2013-05-16
Glad you got it working. :)

---
